intro.title: Past Perfect Simple and Past Perfect Continuous

sections.title: What past perfect does (core logic)
sections.content:
Past perfect = the **past before the past**: in a past-tense story it marks the action that happened **earlier** than another past action.
Same perfect-aspect logic as present perfect, but anchored in the past instead of now (Verb Phrase root, #2 in the Tree Model). Without it, storytelling, news reports, exam transformations and formal writing all blur.

diagrams.text: Czech does **not** distinguish *had done* from *did* the way English does — so Czech students routinely skip past perfect and leave the sequence ambiguous.

sections.title: Core example — order changes the story
sections.content: The same sentence with and without *had* tells two different stories.

items.good: When I arrived, she had already left.
items.why: leaving happened **before** arriving

items.good: When I arrived, she left.
items.why: she left **after** I arrived — a different story

sections.title: Forming past perfect — simple & continuous
sections.content:
Both forms use **had** for every subject (I, you, he, she, we, they) — no agreement to worry about.
*had* is the single past-perfect signal — the rest is mechanical: past participle for completed actions, **been + -ing** for ongoing ones. The same machinery as present perfect, one step back in time.

diagrams.title: Past perfect simple vs continuous — form

items.label: contractions
items.formula: I'd, you'd, she'd, we'd, they'd (= had)

sections.title: When to use past perfect SIMPLE
sections.content:
A **completed action before another past point** — four main uses.
Narrative writing, news, Use of English transformations and reported-speech tasks all hinge on this: picking the earlier action and marking it with *had* is the single highest-leverage move for clear past storytelling.

items.good: She had already left when I arrived. / By the time we got to the station, the train had left.
items.why: earlier of two past actions

items.good: I was tired because I had worked late. / He was angry because someone had taken his bag.
items.why: cause of a past state

items.good: If I had known, I would have called.
items.why: third conditional — see Conditionals

items.good: "I've finished" → She said she had finished.
items.why: reported-speech backshift: present perfect or past simple in direct speech → past perfect

sections.title: Past perfect CONTINUOUS — duration before a past point
sections.content:
Emphasises **ongoing activity leading up to a past moment** — in progress until that point, or just stopped, with visible results.
**Restriction:** no stative verbs (know, believe, own, like, understand) — use the simple: *She had known him for years*, not ~~had been knowing~~.
The tense for the *backstory behind a moment* — what someone had been doing, for how long. Exams test it against past perfect simple.

items.good: She had been studying for two hours when the phone rang.
items.why: activity in progress until the call

items.good: His hands were dirty because he had been repairing the car.
items.why: visible result of the prior activity

items.good: They had been arguing since lunchtime.
items.why: with *since* + starting point

sections.title: Past perfect vs past simple — the choice
sections.content:
When **sequence matters** and isn't obvious: **past perfect for the earlier action**, past simple for the later one.
This single choice — which past event gets *had* — is the most common past-tense error for B1/B2 Czech learners and one of the most-tested points on FCE/CAE Use of English.

items.good: When I arrived, she **had left**.
items.why: she left **before** I arrived

items.good: When I arrived, she **left**.
items.why: she left **after** I arrived — two simple past actions in order

items.good: The film **had already started** when we **got** to the cinema.
items.why: earlier = had started; later = got

steps.q: Conjunction already makes the order clear (after, before, then, as soon as)?
steps.yes: past simple for both — After she finished work, she went home.

steps.q: Earlier of two past events, order not otherwise marked?
steps.yes: past perfect for the earlier + past simple for the later

steps.end: Formal writing still often prefers: After she had finished work…

sections.title: Which past tense? — at a glance
sections.content: The whole choice on one card.

diagrams.title: Which past tense — and which version?
rows: Duration / activity up to a past point
I **had been waiting** for an hour when she arrived.

sections.title: Typical errors — form
sections.content: Past perfect is **had + past participle** — never the past simple form, never *did*.

items.good: I had gone to London.
items.why: past participle, not past simple form

items.good: She had never seen that film.
items.bad: She had never saw that film.
items.why: same rule — past participle

items.good: He was sad because he hadn't passed.
items.bad: He was sad because he had didn't pass.
items.why: negative is *hadn't* + participle, no *did*

sections.title: Typical errors — when (not) to use it
sections.content: High-frequency mistakes in writing tasks, gap-fills and Use of English transformations — fixing them gives immediate visible progress on past-narrative quality.

items.good: When I arrived, she had already left.
items.bad: When I arrived, she already left.
items.why: don't skip past perfect when sequence isn't otherwise marked

items.good: When they arrived, the show had started.
items.bad: When they had arrived, the show had started.
items.why: don't double-mark — only the **earlier** event takes *had*

items.good: I finished yesterday.
items.why: past perfect needs another past anchor — plain past simple is fine

items.good: I have finished now.
items.why: *now* anchors in the present → present perfect

items.good: She had known him for years.
items.bad: She had been knowing him for years.
items.why: stative verb — use the simple

sections.title: Practice roadmap
sections.content:
**Direct practice in this curriculum** (start here after the cards):
- Gap fill — past perfect simple & continuous in context
- Correct the sentence — common errors
- MC — choosing between past perfect, past simple and past continuous

**Next steps**: [[topic:past_simple_present_perfect|Past Simple vs Present Perfect]], [[topic:present_perfect|Present Perfect]], [[topic:conditionals|Conditionals]] (third + mixed use past perfect), [[topic:reported_speech|Reported Speech]] (backshift).

questions.question: 'When she arrived, I _____ for an hour.' (Focus on how long I had been waiting.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: How long before another past event → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: 'When I got there, she _____ home.' (She left before I got there.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Action before another past action → past perfect.

questions.question: 'By 9 o'clock he _____ his homework.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: By + time in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Past perfect = had + past participle. Go → gone.

questions.question: 'She was tired because she _____ well.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Cause in the past (before she was tired) → past perfect.

questions.question: 'When they arrived, the meeting _____.' (The meeting started before they arrived.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect.

gapfill.title: Practice: Past perfect simple and continuous

questions.question: When she arrived, I _____ (wait) for an hour.
questions.explanation: How long before another past event → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: When I got home, my sister _____ (already / go) to bed.
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect simple.

questions.question: By 2020 they _____ (sell) the house.
questions.explanation: By + time in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: She _____ (never / be) to Japan before last year.
questions.explanation: Before a past time → past perfect.

questions.question: When I arrived, the film _____ (already / start).
questions.explanation: Earlier past → past perfect.

questions.question: He was angry because someone _____ (take) his bag.
questions.explanation: Cause in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: I _____ (not / see) her before that day.
questions.explanation: Before a past time → past perfect negative.

questions.question: By the time we got to the station, the train _____ (leave).
questions.explanation: By the time + past → past perfect for the earlier action.

questions.question: She _____ (finish) her work before dinner.
questions.explanation: Before another past time → past perfect.

questions.question: _____ you _____ (eat) when they arrived?
questions.explanation: Past perfect question: Had + subject + past participle.

questions.question: We couldn't get in because we _____ (lose) the key.
questions.explanation: Reason in the past → past perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'When I arrived, she left.' (She left before I arrived.) Correct the sentence.
answers: When I arrived, she had left.
When I arrived, she had left
questions.explanation: Earlier past action → past perfect (had left).

questions.question: Wrong: 'I had went to London in 2019.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I had gone to London in 2019.
questions.explanation: Past perfect needs past participle: go → gone. Or use past simple for a single past event.

questions.question: Wrong: 'By 8 o'clock she has left.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: By + past time → past perfect (had left), not present perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He was sad because he had didn't pass the exam.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He was sad because he hadn't passed the exam.
He was sad because he hadn't passed the exam
questions.explanation: Past perfect negative: had not (hadn't) + past participle. No 'did'.

questions.question: Wrong: 'When they had arrived, the show had started.' Correct the sentence.
answers: When they arrived, the show had started.
When they arrived, the show had started
questions.explanation: Main past event (they arrived) → past simple. Earlier action (show started) → past perfect.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She had never saw that film before.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She had never seen that film before.
She had never seen that film before
questions.explanation: Past perfect: had + past participle. See → seen.

mc.title: Practice: Past Perfect Simple and Continuous (multiple choice)
